period: c. 1638 CE (first sustained settlement) to present
title: Mauritius
summary: A small volcanic island in the western Indian Ocean with no native human population until the seventeenth century — where the Dutch, French, and British successively built a sugar-plantation colony worked by enslaved Africans and then by indentured laborers from India, producing in a few generations an entirely new multi-ethnic society from scratch. Mauritius was also, through the fate of the dodo, one of the first famous, well-documented lessons that human arrival could drive a species to extinction.
hook: When Dutch sailors first stepped ashore on Mauritius in 1598, they found an island that had never seen a human being. No permanent human community lived there. In the forest, a large flightless bird walked up to the sailors without fear. It had no reason to be afraid. Nothing had ever hunted it. The sailors named it the dodo, and within a century of human arrival, it was gone — killed by hunting and, above all, by the pigs, rats, and monkeys that people brought with them. The dodo became the world's first famous extinction: proof that a species could be erased by human hands. But the dodo is only the first of Mauritius's extraordinary stories. This island, empty of people until the 1600s, was then filled — by force and by contract — with enslaved Africans, indentured laborers from India, settlers from Europe and China, producing one of the most dramatic social experiments in modern history: a whole new people, made from scratch, in the space of two centuries.

beginner.thePlace:
Mauritius is a small island in the Indian Ocean, about 2,000 kilometers east of the African coast and close to Madagascar. It is volcanic. Steep mountains rise from the interior. Coral reefs circle the shore. The island is not large — you could drive around its coast in a few hours. But its story is enormous.

What makes Mauritius unusual starts with one fact: no people had ever lived there when Europeans first arrived in the 1500s and 1600s. That was extremely rare. By that period, humans had reached almost every habitable place on Earth. Mauritius was one of the last large islands without any people at all. That emptiness would set the stage for everything that followed.
beginner.physicalWorld:
The island sits on a raised plateau of ancient lava. Its highest peaks reach about 800 meters. Tropical forests once covered most of the land. Streams run down from the hills to the coast. The climate is warm and wet, with heavy rains on the eastern side and drier conditions in the west.

Before people arrived, the island's animals had no experience with predators. That is why the dodo walked calmly toward sailors. Large flightless birds, giant tortoises, and unusual reptiles lived without fear. Before people came, the island had no native land mammals except bats — no rats, pigs, cats, dogs, or monkeys, and no mammal predators. That would change quickly when ships arrived and brought those animals with them, deliberately or by accident.
beginner.theLayers:
The Dutch were the first to settle Mauritius, beginning in 1638, and they named it after Maurice of Nassau, a prince of the House of Orange-Nassau. They cut timber, released pigs and deer for food, and brought enslaved people to labor there. But the colony struggled. The Dutch abandoned it twice and left for good in 1710.

Five years later, in 1715, the French took possession and renamed the island Isle de France. Under French rule, the island was transformed into a major sugar colony. Enslaved people were brought from Madagascar, from Mozambique, and from other parts of East Africa. They cleared the land, planted sugar cane, and worked under brutal conditions on the plantations. The population of the island grew, but almost entirely through forced arrivals.

Britain captured the island during the Napoleonic Wars, in 1810, and kept it. The British restored its original name. They abolished slavery across their empire in the 1830s, and on Mauritius this took effect gradually through the mid-1830s.
beginner.whatItBecame:
After the abolition of slavery, the planters needed a new source of labor. They looked to India. From the late 1830s, a large number of laborers were recruited under contracts known as indenture. These contracts were legally binding and tightly controlled. Workers owed their employers long periods of service under conditions that were exploitative and far from free. They came from various parts of India, crossing the ocean to work the same sugar fields.

Mauritius became, in these years, the central laboratory of the global indenture system. Before the system spread across the Indian Ocean, the Caribbean, and beyond, it was tested and built in Mauritius. The immigration depot at Aapravasi Ghat, in the capital Port Louis, was the first point of arrival for hundreds of thousands of workers. It is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

Scholars estimate the total number of indentured laborers who came to Mauritius across the period of mass indenture at several hundred thousand. Some estimates reach around half a million.
beginner.whoClaimsIt:
From this layered history came the people of Mauritius today. The population is descended from enslaved Africans and Malagasy people, from Indian indentured laborers, from Chinese traders and settlers, and from European colonizers. These groups arrived in a place that had no prior inhabitants and no prior culture. They built new cultures — one, then several — from the languages and memories they carried, and these now share the island.

Mauritius became independent from Britain in 1968. It is a small republic, functioning as a democracy, with a mixed economy. Several languages are spoken, including Creole, French, English, and Bhojpuri. The Creole and Indo-Mauritian communities are the largest. The diversity of the island is not ancient — it was assembled by European empire, across about two centuries, through slavery and coerced labor.
beginner.theLongLife:
The dodo is Mauritius's most famous legacy. It was gone by the late seventeenth century, within decades of human settlement. Its image became, over the following centuries, the symbol of extinction — the proof that humans could erase a species entirely. When people today speak of something as 'dead as a dodo,' they are reaching back to this island and to what happened when the first ships arrived.

Mauritius also showed the world what the indenture system could become. The contracts and institutions built there were copied and exported across the globe. Millions of people in the Indian Ocean world, in the Caribbean, in East Africa, and in Fiji bear descent from workers who passed through systems modeled on what Mauritius pioneered.

And Mauritius stands as proof that a people can be assembled from nothing — that an empty island can, in two centuries, become home to a complex, multilingual, multi-ethnic society. That society's existence is inseparable from the violence and coercion that created it.

general.thePlace:
Mauritius is a small volcanic island in the southwestern Indian Ocean, about 1,865 square kilometers and rising to peaks above 800 meters in the interior. Coral reefs ring the coastline. The island sits roughly 900 kilometers east of Madagascar and about 2,000 kilometers from the African coast.

Its most important fact is also its simplest: no people had ever lived there when Europeans arrived. Portuguese sailors documented it in the early sixteenth century. Dutch sailors landed in 1598. Neither group found any human community. The island held wildlife that had evolved without mammalian predators. The dodo walked toward sailors without fear. It had never learned to be afraid. The colonial project that followed was not the displacement of an existing people. It was the construction of an entirely new society, assembled from populations brought in by force and contract.
general.physicalWorld:
The dodo was a large flightless bird, related to pigeons, that had evolved over millions of years without any predator. Hunters killed some directly. But the animals European settlers released on the island did more damage: introduced swine and rodents ate the eggs, and deer and other grazers competed for the forest floor where dodos nested. By the late seventeenth century, the species was gone.

The dodo's disappearance was the first clearly documented extinction caused by human arrival. It took a long time for this to register as significant. The idea that a species could be permanently erased was uncomfortable in an era when most Europeans believed the natural world was divinely ordered. Over the centuries that followed, the dodo became the standard emblem of irreversible loss. It was gone because people arrived — not because of any failing of the bird itself.
general.theLayers:
The Dutch East India Company established the first settlement in 1638, naming the island Mauritius after Maurice of Nassau. The company used it as a way-station, cut its timber, and brought enslaved workers to labor there. The colony was unprofitable and the Dutch left for good in 1710.

The French arrived in 1715 and renamed the island Isle de France. Over the following century, they built a plantation economy. Enslaved people were transported from Madagascar, Mozambique, and the East African coast. They cleared the forest and planted sugar cane. Port Louis grew into a real Indian Ocean port. When Britain captured the island in 1810 during the Napoleonic Wars, sugar already dominated the economy and the population was primarily enslaved.

Britain governed the island as a Crown Colony. It abolished slavery across its empire in stages during the 1830s. Emancipation in Mauritius was complete by the mid-1830s, though a transitional period delayed full legal freedom.
general.whatItBecame:
After emancipation, many formerly enslaved people refused to continue plantation work. The sugar economy depended on intensive labor, and the planters faced a crisis. Their solution was indenture. From 1834 onward, they recruited workers from India under contracts that bound employees to specific employers, usually for five years, and restricted their movement. Marina Carter's research documents what the system looked like in practice: overcrowded ships, poor food, inadequate housing on the estates, and a legal system that enforced the contract against workers far more reliably than it protected them. Indenture was not slavery — workers had legal personhood and could not be bought or sold. But it was coercive in practice.

Mauritius was where this system was first built at scale, and from Mauritius it spread across the British Empire. The same contracts, routes, and administrative apparatus were deployed in the Caribbean, East Africa, and Fiji. The immigration depot at Aapravasi Ghat in Port Louis, where workers disembarked, is now a UNESCO World Heritage Site — recognized as the founding location of a system that eventually shaped millions of lives.
general.whoClaimsIt:
The population of Mauritius today descends from enslaved Africans and Malagasy people, Indian indentured laborers, Chinese traders, and European settlers. No community was there first. Each arrived to an island without prior inhabitants and built its own world within a shared one, under conditions of very unequal power.

Mauritius became independent in 1968. It has held free elections since and maintained civilian governance across changes in government. Indo-Mauritians are now the demographic majority. Creoles — descendants of the enslaved — form the second-largest group. The Creole language is the everyday tongue of most Mauritians: a French-based language with African, Malagasy, and Indian words woven through it, a genuine product of the encounter between all the groups who arrived here.
general.theLongLife:
Almost every other colonial history involves the transformation of an existing society. Mauritius is unusual: there was nothing to transform. The population, the crops, the social institutions, the landscape — all were imported by colonial power and assembled across roughly two centuries. The result was a new people, formed faster than almost anywhere in the modern world.

That this society emerged from slavery and coerced labor, and yet became a functioning multi-ethnic democracy, is the central puzzle Mauritius poses. Historians disagree about how to weigh the suffering that built the island against what grew from it. Both the coercion and the belonging are real, and neither cancels the other.

advanced.thePlace:
Mauritius poses an interpretive problem that most colonial histories are not designed to engage: what does it mean for a colonial project to encounter no prior society at all? The standard colonial history rests on the concept of dispossession — the removal or displacement of an existing population and the seizure of their land. On Mauritius that frame applies only in an attenuated way. There were no prior inhabitants to dispossess. The land was genuinely uninhabited. The colonial project here was not one of dispossession but of fabrication: the construction, from imported labor and capital, of an entirely new society on a blank site.

This distinction has consequences for analysis. The plantation colony at Mauritius did not overlay or destroy an existing culture. It built one, from raw materials imported from Africa, India, China, and Europe, in conditions of radical power asymmetry. The enslaved people and indentured workers who came did not lose an ancestral homeland through the Mauritian colonial project — they had already been removed from their homelands before arriving. Their dispossession happened elsewhere: in the slave raids on the East African coast, in the recruitment drives through Bihar and Madras. What Mauritius offers is the downstream end of those processes, concentrated on a small island where the constructive logic of the plantation complex is unusually visible.
advanced.physicalWorld:
The dodo's extinction poses a methodological problem that recurs throughout environmental history: the evidence for causation is structured by the limitations of what the people who caused the damage bothered to record. The claim that introduced species — pigs, rats, monkeys — were more destructive than direct hunting rests primarily on inference from the extinction pattern rather than direct documentation. No account by a sailor or colonist systematically records egg predation or habitat loss. Subfossil remains establish the dodo's presence before human arrival and its absence afterward, but the granular timeline of population collapse cannot be recovered from surviving evidence. The species survived millions of years of isolation without significant predation pressure and disappeared within decades of a regime change that introduced multiple simultaneous pressures. Disentangling which pressure mattered most is not possible from what survives.

What is analytically interesting about the dodo's reception history is how slowly its disappearance registered as a loss at all. Contemporary accounts treated the bird as a curiosity, sometimes a nuisance. The transformation of the dodo into a philosophical emblem — the canonical proof that humans could permanently eliminate a species — was a retrospective construction, built over the following two centuries as naturalists gradually assembled the concept of anthropogenic extinction. The dodo did not become a symbol of human destructiveness until long after it was gone. That delay is itself a finding about how ecological awareness develops.
advanced.theLayers:
The French plantation economy of Isle de France has been examined primarily through its economic and demographic dimensions: the growth of sugar production, the scale of the slave trade to the island, and the ethnic and legal categories that structured the colonial population. Vijayalakshmi Teelock's work on Mauritian slavery has documented the conditions under which enslaved people lived and worked, the diversity of origins among the enslaved population, and the ways in which resistance and accommodation structured plantation life. The slave population on Mauritius was not a monolith — it included people from many different East African and Malagasy backgrounds, with different languages and cultural practices, who did not share a prior common identity. The Creole culture and language that emerged were genuinely new formations.

The British conquest of 1810 and the subsequent transition to indenture deserve closer scrutiny than they sometimes receive. The British did not abolish the plantation system; they preserved it. Emancipation in the 1830s was followed almost immediately by the large-scale substitution of indentured labor. The continuity of plantation production across this political and legal rupture is one of the most important analytical facts about Mauritius — it demonstrates that the shift from enslaved to indentured labor was, in its immediate economic function, a mechanism for maintaining the plantation system rather than dismantling it. Richard Allen's comparative work on labor in the Indian Ocean world addresses this continuity directly.
advanced.whatItBecame:
The analytic distinction between chattel slavery and indentured labor is real and must not be erased: the indentured worker retained legal personhood, could not be sold, and could in principle bring legal complaints. But the scholarly literature has moved away from treating this distinction as fully separable in practice. Marina Carter's detailed studies of Mauritian indenture document that the legal apparatus consistently favored employers, that workers who broke contract terms faced criminal penalties, and that the freedoms nominally available under indenture were frequently unavailable in practice. The result was a system that was legally distinct from slavery but that shared with it the core characteristic of non-voluntary, coerced labor extracting surplus value from workers who had been removed from their home societies and placed in a dependency relationship with no practical exit.

The term 'a new system of slavery,' applied to indenture by some contemporaneous abolitionists, has been debated by historians ever since. The debate is not merely terminological. It concerns the historical relationship between the formal abolition of slavery and the continuation of labor coercion, and it bears on how we understand the arc of liberal empire in the nineteenth century. Mauritius is the primary site of that debate because indenture began there earliest and operated there at the largest scale relative to the island's total population.
advanced.whoClaimsIt:
The plural society of modern Mauritius is simultaneously the most visible legacy of the plantation and indenture systems and a genuine achievement of a kind that the colonial project did not intend. Colonial planters did not assemble enslaved Africans and indentured Indians to create a multicultural democracy; they did so to produce sugar. The democracy that emerged, and the cultural pluralism that characterizes contemporary Mauritian life, were generated by people who had been brought to the island against their will or under coercive contracts and who built, over generations, communities and institutions that eventually claimed political self-determination.

The ethnic politics of post-independence Mauritius have been structured by the colonial categories. The Indo-Mauritian majority, the Creole community (descended from the enslaved), the Sino-Mauritian community, and the Franco-Mauritian elite have engaged in complex negotiations over representation, economic access, and cultural recognition. The 1968 independence settlement formalized proportional representation by communal group, a structure that both acknowledged the plural character of the society and embedded communal categories into the political system.
advanced.theLongLife:
Mauritius is one of a small number of sites where the full arc of what historians call the second slavery — the plantation complex as it extended and transformed from the eighteenth into the nineteenth century — can be read with unusual clarity. The island shows the transition from directly enslaved labor to legally coerced but nominally free labor, the transfer of that system globally, and the long-run social consequences in the form of a diaspora society that still carries the marks of its origins.

The island's analytical value for global historians is its compactness and its clarity. Everything was imported; nothing was indigenous. The colonial project is visible in pure form because there is no prior society to muddy the picture. That same clarity is what makes the dodo so powerful as a symbol: an island with no human history, encountering humans, and losing within decades both the bird that had evolved in its isolation and the possibility of that isolation returning.

foundation.theStory: Mauritius presents a historical problem unusual in its clarity: an island with no prior human population that was transformed, within two centuries, into a plantation colony and then into a multi-ethnic society. The historical questions it raises are not primarily about what was destroyed — there was no prior human culture to destroy — but about what was constructed, by what means, and at what cost. The story runs through the extinction of the dodo (the first recorded anthropogenic extinction), the French slave-plantation economy, the British transition from slavery to indenture, and the emergence of a new Creole-and-Indo-Mauritian society. Working through Mauritius means working at the intersection of environmental history, the history of Atlantic and Indian Ocean slavery, the history of labor systems under colonialism, and the history of diaspora society formation.
foundation.theDebate: Two clusters of debate organize scholarly work on Mauritius. First, the relationship between enslaved and indentured labor: was indenture a genuinely new labor system or a continuation of coercive plantation labor under a different legal form? Historians including Marina Carter argue that the distinction matters legally but that the coercive character of indentured labor was sufficiently similar in practice to warrant the term 'new system of slavery' that abolitionists applied to it at the time. Others emphasize the legal personhood retained by indentured workers and the real, if constrained, paths to mobility that indenture eventually permitted. Second, the scholarly debate about the dodo and early modern extinction: to what degree was hunting versus introduced species the primary cause, and what does the answer imply about the category of anthropogenic extinction as it applies to early colonial contact?
foundation.whyItMatters: Mauritius unlocks several important analytical domains simultaneously. It is the clearest site for studying the transition from slave to indenture labor in the British Empire, a transition that exported a new labor system across much of the world. It is the founding case of documented anthropogenic extinction. It is an example of a diaspora society — a population assembled entirely from migration and coercion — that achieved political independence and democratic self-governance. Understanding Mauritius provides purchase on the Indian Ocean slave trade, the global plantation system, the history of labor coercion after abolition, and the ecological consequences of human arrival on isolated islands.
questions: The indenture system that began in Mauritius was applied across the British Empire after abolition. In what ways did indenture resemble chattel slavery in its practical operation, and in what ways was it genuinely different? What does the answer to that question imply about how we should understand the abolition of slavery as a historical event?

The dodo's extinction happened within decades of human settlement. The causes included both hunting and the introduction of non-native animals. Why does the question of which cause predominated matter beyond academic interest — and what does the dodo's case reveal about how we assign responsibility for environmental destruction?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The scholarly literature on Mauritius divides across several domains that rarely converge. On indenture and labor history, Marina Carter's work — particularly Servants, Sirdars and Settlers (1995) — is the foundational study of the Mauritian indenture system, drawing on estate records, colonial archives, and legal case files to document conditions, resistance, and the structure of the indenture apparatus. Richard Allen's Slaves, Freedmen and Indentured Laborers in Colonial Mauritius (1999) provides the comparative economic and demographic framework. On the slavery period, Vijayalakshmi Teelock's Bitter Sugar: Sugar and Slavery in 19th Century Mauritius (1998) is the central treatment. On the dodo, Errol Fuller's Dodo: From Extinction to Icon (2002) traces both the bird's biology and the cultural history of how the dodo became the canonical emblem of human-caused extinction. The UNESCO designation of Aapravasi Ghat has generated additional scholarly and policy literature on the material heritage of the indenture system.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidentiary base for Mauritius's history is uneven. For the indenture period, estate records, immigration depot records, and colonial government archives survive in substantial volume, allowing detailed reconstruction of labor conditions, demographic flows, and legal disputes. For the slavery period, plantation records are more fragmentary, and the voices of the enslaved are largely absent from the surviving documentation. For the Dutch period and the dodo, the primary evidence consists of ships' logs, traveler accounts, and skeletal remains: detailed enough to establish the broad story but not granular enough to settle questions of causation. The precise extinction date of the dodo, for example, cannot be established from surviving evidence — the last reliable firsthand account dates from 1662, but absence of subsequent sightings is not the same as confirmed extinction at that moment.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A defensible scholarly position on Mauritius's significance must hold together two claims that are sometimes treated as competing. The first is that the indenture system was genuinely coercive and exploitative and cannot be normalized as a benign labor alternative to slavery simply because it was legally distinct. The second is that the outcomes of the indenture system were not uniform — some workers achieved mobility, some communities built durable institutions, and the Indo-Mauritian society that emerged is not simply a victim community but a complex social formation with its own history of agency. A strong argument engages both the structural coercion of the system and the historical agency of the people who worked within and against it. It also situates Mauritius within the global export of the indenture system — understanding the island requires treating it not just as a local story but as the origin point of a model applied to millions of people across several continents.
questions: Marina Carter's research draws on estate records, immigration depot files, and court records to reconstruct the daily operation of the indenture system. What does the use of these particular sources enable historians to see — and what dimensions of workers' experience do these administrative records necessarily fail to capture?

Mauritius is described as a 'made-from-scratch society' — a population assembled entirely through migration, slavery, and indenture, with no indigenous community. What analytical challenges does this origin pose for a society trying to develop a national identity and collective memory after independence?

research.topic: The sequence of colonial settlement and the indenture system's Mauritian origins
research.content: It is well established that Mauritius had no indigenous human population before European contact; that the Dutch settled it from 1638 and named it after Maurice of Nassau; that the French took possession in 1715 as Isle de France and built a sugar plantation economy worked by enslaved Africans and Malagasy people; that the British conquered the island in 1810 during the Napoleonic Wars; that British abolition of slavery took effect on the island in the mid-1830s; and that large-scale recruitment of indentured laborers from India began from the mid-1830s. The immigration depot at Aapravasi Ghat in Port Louis, recognized by UNESCO, is the documented point of arrival for the system that was subsequently exported across the British Empire. Mauritius achieved independence in 1968.

research.topic: Conditions and coercion in the Mauritian indenture system
research.content: The scholarly literature on the practical conditions of Mauritian indenture has expanded substantially since Marina Carter's archival work in the 1990s, drawing on estate records, immigration depot files, and colonial court records. Emerging research has used these materials to document resistance strategies, the role of women in the indenture system, and the internal diversity of experience among Indian workers of different regional, caste, and religious backgrounds. The question of how to characterize indenture relative to slavery — and specifically whether the formal legal distinction between the two systems corresponded to a meaningful practical difference in lived conditions — remains an active area of scholarly discussion. Comparative work linking Mauritian indenture to its subsequent forms in other British colonies has also grown.

research.topic: The precise timing and primary causes of the dodo's extinction
research.content: What evidence would resolve the question: direct observational records of dodo population decline across the period of settlement, combined with systematic documentation of the distribution and impact of introduced species (pigs, rats, monkeys) across the island, would establish both the timeline and the relative contribution of each cause. Why that evidence is unavailable: no such systematic naturalist observation was carried out; the island was visited and recorded by sailors and colonists whose interests were commercial and navigational rather than ecological; and the bird's disappearance was not recognized as a significant event at the time it occurred. What can be responsibly inferred: the dodo was present in the 1640s through 1660s based on firsthand accounts and was absent from reliable accounts by the end of the seventeenth century; hunting is documented; introduced pigs and rats are documented as present in the same period; the combination of direct predation by hunters, egg predation by introduced animals, and habitat disturbance was sufficient to drive a low-density, slow-reproducing flightless bird to extinction. What must remain unknowable: the precise date of the last individual, the relative weight of hunting versus introduced species as causes, and whether the extinction was a rapid pulse or a prolonged decline cannot be established from surviving evidence.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Descendants of the enslaved and Creole Mauritians
comparativeNarrative.name: The Missing History
comparativeNarrative.content: For the Creole community descended from enslaved Africans and Malagasy people, the standard story of Mauritius as a multicultural success obscures a specific history of loss. The enslaved built the sugar economy. After emancipation, they were displaced from plantation work by indentured Indian workers, who arrived under contracts that kept wages low. Creoles argue that this sequence — building the economy, then being pushed aside — explains their community's relative disadvantage today. The commemoration landscape reflects that tension. Aapravasi Ghat, the indenture depot, has international UNESCO recognition. Le Morne Brabant, a site tied to Africans who escaped slavery by hiding in caves, has recently received comparable recognition. From this perspective, calling Mauritius a 'made-from-scratch' society erases the specific people who were brought there first and what they suffered.

comparativeNarrative.perspective: Indo-Mauritian community and indenture descendant societies
comparativeNarrative.name: Arrival and Belonging
comparativeNarrative.content: For descendants of Indian indentured laborers — in Mauritius and across the wider diaspora that reached Trinidad, Guyana, Fiji, and South Africa — the island marks the start of a specific migrant experience. Workers came under contracts that bound them for years to employers they had not chosen, in a place far from home. The word 'Girmitiya,' derived from the word for 'agreement,' names this experience and carries its own weight in diaspora memory. In Mauritius, the Indo-Mauritian community is now the demographic majority. But majority status has not erased the memory of arriving as coerced labor. The recognition of Aapravasi Ghat as a World Heritage Site gave this history formal standing in global memory — something that took until 2006 to achieve.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Ecological Rupture
comparativeNarrative.content: For natural historians and ecologists, Mauritius is above all the place where humans first demonstrably wiped out a species. The dodo is the famous case, but it was not alone. Giant tortoises disappeared. Several bird species vanished. Endemic plants were lost. The plantation economy that followed did not just use the island — it remade it. Forest was cleared for cane fields. Wetlands were drained. Hundreds of non-native species were introduced. The original ecosystem, which had evolved over millions of years without human interference, was largely replaced within a few centuries. Conservation programs in the late twentieth century have restored some species to offshore islands around Mauritius. That recovery is real. But most of what existed before 1638 is gone.

comparativeSummary: Mauritius appears quite differently depending on which strand of its history is centered. For Creole Mauritians descended from the enslaved, the island's story is one of a specific community whose foundational labor was later displaced and whose suffering has been unevenly recognized in national and international commemoration. For Indo-Mauritian descendants of indentured workers, it is the origin of a diaspora experience defined by coerced migration and the construction of new communities in alien conditions. For environmental historians and ecologists, it is the first great site of human-caused extinction, where a predator-free world was fatally disrupted by a single generation of colonial settlement. No single perspective is sufficient: the dodo's extinction, the slavery period, and the indenture system are not separate stories — they are consecutive chapters of what happens when colonial power encounters an uninhabited island and fills it, with force, with the materials of a plantation economy.

rabbitHole.label: Madagascar
rabbitHole.reason: Madagascar offers a direct contrast with Mauritius: it is a vast island settled by humans roughly a thousand years before the Dutch reached Mauritius, with its own ancient peoples, languages, and ecology. Mauritius had no people at all until the 1600s. The comparison sharpens what is distinctive about Mauritius — its complete lack of an indigenous population — and the very different colonial histories that resulted from each island's pre-contact situation.

rabbitHole.label: Sugar
rabbitHole.reason: Sugar was the plantation crop that drove everything on Mauritius — the reason for enslaved labor, the reason for indenture, the reason for the island's economic value to successive colonial powers. The political economy of sugar explains why Mauritius was transformed as it was.

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: The Dutch, French, and British colonial projects on Mauritius were expressions of the broader logic of European empire in the Indian Ocean. Understanding the institutional and ideological structures of colonialism is the analytical frame within which the Mauritian plantation economy, slavery, and indenture operated.

rabbitHole.label: The Transatlantic Slave Trade
rabbitHole.reason: The enslaved people brought to Mauritius came primarily from East Africa and Madagascar rather than through the Atlantic system, but the plantation logic — sugar worked by enslaved labor — is the same. The comparison between the Atlantic and Indian Ocean slave trades illuminates both the shared structure and the specific regional character of Mauritian slavery.

rabbitHole.label: Indian Ocean Trade
rabbitHole.reason: Mauritius became significant because the Indian Ocean trade system made it a strategic location — a way-station on routes between Europe, Africa, and Asia. The same ocean world that structured trade also structured the labor flows of enslaved Africans and Indian indentured workers that made the island's plantation economy possible.

rabbitHole.label: The Indian Ocean Slave Trade
rabbitHole.reason: The enslaved people who worked the French plantation economy on Isle de France came through the Indian Ocean slave trade, drawn from Madagascar, Mozambique, and the East African coast. Mauritius was one of the major plantation destinations of this system, and understanding the trade illuminates who was enslaved, how they were transported, and what their lives on the island involved.

rabbitHole.label: The Plantation
rabbitHole.reason: The plantation was the organizing institution of Mauritius under both French and British rule — the form of enterprise that structured land, labor, and social hierarchy on the island across two centuries. Mauritius is one of the clearest cases of the plantation as a total social system.

reference.author: Marina Carter
reference.title: Servants, Sirdars and Settlers: Indians in Mauritius 1834–1874
reference.annotation: The foundational scholarly study of the Mauritian indenture system in its first four decades, drawing on estate records, immigration depot archives, and colonial legal files. Carter documents labor conditions, the structure of the indenture apparatus, resistance strategies, and the daily lives of Indian workers on Mauritian sugar estates. The entry's treatment of indenture as a coercive system with specific legal and material dimensions draws directly on this work.

reference.author: Richard B. Allen
reference.title: Slaves, Freedmen and Indentured Laborers in Colonial Mauritius
reference.annotation: Provides the comparative economic and demographic framework for understanding labor in Mauritius from the slave period through the indenture era. Allen's work situates Mauritius within the broader Indian Ocean labor history and directly addresses the continuity of plantation production across the legal transition from slavery to indenture — a central analytical point in this entry.

reference.author: Errol Fuller
reference.title: Dodo: From Extinction to Icon
reference.annotation: The most comprehensive account of the dodo, covering its biology, the evidence for its extinction, the history of its representation in art and science, and its transformation into the canonical symbol of human-caused extinction. Fuller's careful treatment of the evidentiary limits — what is established, what is uncertain, what cannot be known — is the basis for the entry's humility on the dodo's extinction date and primary causes.

reference.author: Vijayalakshmi Teelock
reference.title: Bitter Sugar: Sugar and Slavery in 19th Century Mauritius
reference.annotation: Documents the conditions of enslaved people on Mauritian plantations, the diversity of African and Malagasy origins among the enslaved population, and the forms of resistance and accommodation that characterized plantation life. Teelock's work is the primary scholarly treatment of the slavery period that preceded indenture and provides the basis for the entry's account of who was enslaved and under what conditions.

reference.author: Hugh Tinker
reference.title: A New System of Slavery: The Export of Indian Labour Overseas 1830–1920
reference.annotation: The foundational comparative study of Indian indentured labor across the British Empire, establishing the framework within which Mauritius is understood as the origin point of a system that spread globally. Tinker coined the term 'new system of slavery' that has structured scholarly debate about indenture ever since. The entry's framing of Mauritius as a laboratory for a system exported worldwide draws on this work's analytical architecture.

commerce.title: Servants, Sirdars and Settlers: Indians in Mauritius 1834–1874
commerce.author: Marina Carter
commerce.note: The place to go if you want the full scholarly account of indenture in Mauritius — Carter's archival research is dense but precise, and it is the book that any serious engagement with the system must pass through. Best read after getting the broader context from a more accessible work.

commerce.title: Dodo: From Extinction to Icon
commerce.author: Errol Fuller
commerce.note: If the dodo is what draws you to Mauritius, Fuller is the right first book — meticulous about what the evidence actually shows versus what is legend, it follows the bird from its evolutionary origins through extinction and into its cultural afterlife, and it is the most complete single account of the dodo story available to a general reader.

commerce.title: Slaves, Freedmen and Indentured Laborers in Colonial Mauritius
commerce.author: Richard B. Allen
commerce.note: Read this alongside Carter for the comparative and demographic framework — Allen situates Mauritius within the broader Indian Ocean labor system and is the clearest guide to the economic continuities between the slave and indenture periods.

commerce.title: A History of Mauritius
commerce.author: Auguste Toussaint
commerce.note: Toussaint's history provides the broadest chronological sweep across the island's colonial and post-colonial history for a reader who wants narrative context before diving into the specialist literature on indenture or slavery.

commerce.title: Bitter Sugar: Sugar and Slavery in 19th Century Mauritius
commerce.author: Vijayalakshmi Teelock
commerce.note: Read this for the slavery period specifically — Teelock's account of who was enslaved, from where, and under what conditions is the clearest guide to the foundation on which the indenture system was later built.

commerce.title: Paul et Virginie
commerce.author: Bernardin de Saint-Pierre
commerce.note: Saint-Pierre's idyll, set on the French sugar colony where he lived, helped fix the island in Europe's imagination. Read it for the early colonial Mauritius beneath the romance — slavery shadows the pastoral.

commerce.title: The Last Brother
commerce.author: Nathacha Appanah
commerce.note: Appanah, a Mauritian writer, turns to the Jewish refugees interned on the island during the war, seen through a local boy. Read it for the island as an unlikely crossroads of other peoples' histories.

popularCulture.title: Dead as a dodo
popularCulture.description: The phrase 'dead as a dodo' is used worldwide to mean something completely gone and never coming back. Most people who say it do not know it refers to Mauritius. Popular accounts often call the dodo stupid. In fact the bird had simply never met a predator. The phrase blames the bird for its own death. The real story is about what humans brought with them.

popularCulture.title: Natural history documentaries on the dodo (BBC and National Geographic)
popularCulture.description: Nature films have covered the dodo's extinction many times. They focus on the ecological story — a fearless bird, then human arrival, then loss. The plantation history that followed on the same island rarely appears. The enslaved people and indentured workers who built the sugar economy stay off screen. The island shows up as a nature story, not a human one.

popularCulture.title: The Dodo and the Solitaire: A Natural History
popularCulture.description: Jolyon Parish's book is careful about what the evidence actually shows. It pushes back on the popular image of the dodo as clumsy and dim. The book is good on what the bird was and how it lived. It says less about the plantation colony that grew on the same island after the dodo vanished. The natural history and the human history rarely meet in the same account.

popularCulture.title: Aapravasi Ghat and diaspora memory
popularCulture.description: When UNESCO named Aapravasi Ghat a World Heritage Site in 2006, it gave a history that had been mostly invisible a formal place in global memory. For descendants of Indian indentured workers in Trinidad, Guyana, Fiji, and South Africa, the site matters personally. It names an experience — crossing the ocean under contract to work a sugar field — that shaped millions of lives and was rarely taught in schools.

popularCulture.title: Mauritius as a model plural society
popularCulture.description: Mauritius is sometimes cited as proof that a deeply mixed society can work. The island has many languages, many religions, and several ethnic communities. It has held free elections since 1968. That story is true but incomplete. The mixing came from slavery and forced labor. The harmony that followed took generations of hard work to build on very unequal foundations.
